theme.theme-annotation: Blue study theme — cool slate and cerulean palette. Clean, calm, and considered. Evokes clarity of thought, open skies, and still water. Suited to studies with a reflective or systematic character. Professional without being cold.

elements.text:
"The gospel is not good advice to men, but good news about Christ; not an invitation for us to do anything, but a declaration of what God has done.
— John Stott

We never move on from the gospel; we move on in the gospel.
— Timothy Keller

elements.question:
What do you think of the above quotations?
(Do you like them or not, and why?)
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Many people assume the gospel is a set of instructions – a guide to living a better life, a moral standard to aim for, or a list of things God expects from you.


But the word simply means good news. And news, by definition, is something that has already happened. The gospel is not advice about what you must do. It is an announcement about what God has done in Jesus Christ – his death, burial, and resurrection – on behalf of people who could not save themselves.


That distinction matters enormously. Advice puts the weight on you. News invites a response to something already accomplished.

elements.text: What is the Gospel?
The gospel is good news – not advice about what we must do, but news about what God has done.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>For I passed on to you as of first importance what I also received – that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures, <sup>4</sup>and that he was buried, and that he was raised on the third day according to the scriptures.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel has a specific content that can be defined and passed on: Christ died for our sins (dealing with the problem of sin), he was buried (confirming his real death), and he was raised on the third day (confirming his victory over death). Paul calls this ’of first importance’ – it is not one Christian idea among many but the central, non-negotiable message. It is also grounded in Scripture, meaning it was the fulfilment of God’s long-standing plan, not an unexpected event.
elements.questionHint: Paul says this message is ’of first importance’ – what three historical facts does he list, and what problem does the first one address?

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel is necessary for everyone, without exception. The word ’all’ leaves no room for anyone to claim they are good enough on their own. Sin is not just about outward behaviour; it means falling short of God’s glory – his perfect standard. This establishes the problem that the gospel comes to solve: every person is in need of rescue, which is why the good news of Christ is relevant to all people.
elements.questionHint: The verse uses the word ’all’ – what does that tell you about who the gospel is for, and why it is needed?

elements.passageText: <sup>23</sup>For the payoff of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The verse presents a stark contrast: sin earns death (a wage that is deserved), but eternal life is a gift (something unearned and freely given). This contrast is at the heart of the gospel – we deserve one thing, but through Christ God offers the opposite. The word ’gift’ is crucial: eternal life cannot be worked for or earned; it can only be received. The fact that it comes ’in Christ Jesus’ shows that this gift is inseparably connected to him.
elements.questionHint: Notice the contrast between ’payoff’ (something earned) and ’gift’ (something given freely) – what does that difference tell us about how we receive eternal life?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Grace is often understood as God being lenient – turning a blind eye, or lowering the bar for people who are trying their best. But grace isn’t a reduced standard. It’s an undeserved gift.


The key word is undeserved. Grace is not God rewarding the almost-good-enough. It is God acting generously toward people who had nothing to offer and no claim to make. Ephesians 2:8–9 puts it plainly: salvation is ’by grace, through faith – and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God.’


Grace isn’t God grading on a curve. It’s God giving a gift that could never be earned.

elements.text: The Gospel is about Jesus. It is not centred on us—it is centred on him. (It is for us, but it is about him.)

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; <sup>9</sup>it is not from works, so that no one can boast.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Salvation comes entirely from God, not from human effort. It is by grace (God’s undeserved favour), received through faith (trusting in what God has done, not what we do). Paul explicitly rules out works as the basis of salvation, and gives the reason: if we could earn it, we would have grounds to boast. The fact that no one can boast points to salvation being 100% God’s doing. This protects the gospel from becoming a self-improvement programme and keeps it focused on what God has given.
elements.questionHint: Paul says salvation is ’not from yourselves’ and ’not from works’ – what does he say it actually is, and why does that distinction matter?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>For this is the way God loved the world: He gave his one and only Son, so that everyone who believes in him will not perish but have eternal life.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel originates in God’s love for the whole world – it is not an afterthought or a reluctant plan. The depth of that love is shown in what it cost: God gave his ’one and only Son’. The scope is universal (‘the world’, ‘everyone who believes’), meaning no one is excluded by background. The outcome is also clear: belief leads to eternal life rather than perishing. The gospel is therefore a love-driven rescue mission, initiated by God, at great personal cost, available to all.
elements.questionHint: What does the phrase ’he gave his one and only Son’ tell you about how much this cost God, and what does ’everyone who believes’ tell you about who can receive it?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>But God demonstrates his own love for us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from this verse?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s love is not conditional on us first becoming better people. Christ died for us ’while we were still sinners’ – at our worst, not our best. This is what makes the gospel so radical: it is not a reward for progress but a rescue for the undeserving. It also means God’s love is proven through an action (the death of Christ), not just a feeling or a declaration. The gospel reveals a God who moves toward us in our failure rather than waiting for us to earn his favour.
elements.questionHint: Pay attention to the timing in this verse – at what point did Christ die for us, and what does that tell you about the nature of God’s love?

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>For we too were once foolish, disobedient, misled, enslaved to various passions and desires, spending our lives in evil and envy, hateful and hating one another. <sup>4</sup>But when the kindness of God our Savior and his love for mankind appeared, <sup>5</sup>he saved us not by works of righteousness that we have done but on the basis of his mercy, through the washing of the new birth and the renewing of the Holy Spirit, <sup>6</sup>whom he poured out on us in full measure through Jesus Christ our Savior. <sup>7</sup>And so, since we have been justified by his grace, we become heirs with the confident expectation of eternal life. <sup>8</sup>This saying is trustworthy, and I want you to insist on these things, so that those who have accepted God may be intent on engaging in good works. These things are good and beneficial for all people.

elements.question: What do we learn about the Gospel from these verses?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The passage shows both where we were (foolish, disobedient, enslaved to desires) and what God did about it. Salvation came not because of anything we did, but entirely on the basis of God’s mercy, through new birth and renewal by the Holy Spirit. The result is justification by grace and the status of heirs with the hope of eternal life. Crucially, verse 8 shows that good works flow from the gospel as a response – they are the fruit of being saved, not the means of being saved. The Trinity is also involved: saved through Jesus, renewed by the Spirit, on the basis of the Father’s mercy.
elements.questionHint: Verse 5 says ’not by works of righteousness that we have done’ – so what basis does Paul say salvation actually rests on, and where do good works fit in according to verse 8?

elements.text:
The gospel tells us:
  • God created us to know him, love him, and glorify him.
  • We sinned, choosing our own way. The result is separation from God, guilt, shame, foolishness, death, and judgment.
  • God sent his Son Jesus: He lived the perfect life we failed to live, died the death we deserved, and rose again to bring us forgiveness and new life.
  • We are called to respond in repentance and faith – turning from sin and trusting in Jesus as Saviour and Lord.

    • The gospel is the centre of our faith. Everything else in the Christian life flows from this good news. So as a church our purpose is not to entertain ourselves, or make everything comfortable for ourselves. We want to reach people with the good news of the gospel – even when that is hard work, even when it makes things uncomfortable(!)
Our Church Vision arises from the Gospel
We are a community of believers, rooted in Scripture, growing in Christlikeness, and reaching our world with the hope of the gospel.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and exhorting one another with all wisdom, singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, all with grace in your hearts to God. <sup>17</sup>And whatever you do in word or deed, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him.

elements.question: How does God’s Word shape the life of the church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s Word is meant to ’dwell richly’ in the gathered community – not just to be heard occasionally, but to saturate the life of the church. This shapes how believers relate to one another (teaching and exhorting each other with wisdom), how they worship (singing with grace in their hearts), and how they live in every area (doing everything in the name of Jesus with thanksgiving). The Word is not just for private devotion; it is a communal shaping force that gives the church its direction, its relationships, and its everyday culture.
elements.questionHint: What does it mean for the Word to ’dwell richly’ rather than just ’be present’ – and what three different areas of church life does Paul say it affects in these two verses?

elements.passageText: <sup>29</sup>because those whom he foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that his Son would be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters.

elements.question: What is God’s purpose for believers?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s ultimate purpose for every believer is to be conformed to the image of his Son – to become more and more like Jesus in character, values, and love. This is not an optional spiritual upgrade; it is the goal God predestined for his people. The vision of Jesus as ’the firstborn among many brothers and sisters’ shows that this is a family resemblance: we are being shaped to look like the elder brother. Discipleship is therefore not just learning information about Jesus but being transformed into his likeness.
elements.questionHint: The verse says believers are predestined to be ’conformed to the image of his Son’ – what does that suggest about what the goal of the Christian life actually is?

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, <sup>20</sup>teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you. And remember, I am with you always, to the end of the age.

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ’disciple’ can sound like a level of Christianity reserved for the especially committed – people who go on mission trips, lead Bible studies, or have memorised large portions of Scripture.


But in the New Testament, disciple is simply the normal word for a follower of Jesus. There is no category of Christian who is not a disciple. To believe in Jesus is to follow him – and to follow him is to be, by definition, his disciple.


What varies is not whether we are disciples, but how closely and faithfully we are following. That is what this study is about.

elements.question: What is our mission as a church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The church’s mission is to make disciples of all nations – a task that involves going out, baptising new believers (bringing them into the community of faith), and teaching them to obey everything Jesus commanded. The scope is universal (‘all nations’), the content is comprehensive (‘everything I have commanded’), and the method is relational and formational, not just informational. Jesus also provides the motivation and foundation: his constant presence (‘I am with you always’) means this mission is not carried out in our own strength.
elements.questionHint: Jesus gives one central command in these verses – what is it, and what two actions does he describe as part of how it gets done?

elements.text: Our Tagline: Make Many Deep Disciples

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>He said to them, ‘Follow me, and I will turn you into fishers of people.’

elements.question: What did Jesus call His first disciples to do?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus called his first disciples to follow him personally, and promised that as they did, he would transform them into ’fishers of people’ – people who bring others to him. There are two things worth noting: first, the call is to follow Jesus himself, not just to adopt a set of beliefs. Second, reaching others is presented as the natural outcome of following Jesus, not a separate programme. As disciples grow in their relationship with Jesus, they become people who draw others in.
elements.questionHint: Jesus links two things in this verse: following him and becoming fishers of people – what does the connection between those two things suggest about how mission flows from discipleship?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>Therefore, just as you received Christ Jesus as Lord, continue to live your lives in him, <sup>7</sup>rooted and built up in him and firm in your faith just as you were taught, and overflowing with thankfulness.

elements.question: What should mark a ’deep disciple’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: A deep disciple is someone who continues to live in Christ in the same way they first received him – through faith and trust, not through self-achievement. Paul uses three vivid images: being rooted (drawing life from Christ, like a tree from soil), being built up (growing in substance and stability), and being firm in faith (not easily shaken or misled). Overflowing thankfulness is also highlighted – a deep disciple is not someone who has become grim or self-reliant, but someone whose life is marked by gratitude for what God has done.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses images of roots, building, and firmness – what kind of qualities do those images suggest, and what emotion does he say should overflow in a growing disciple?

elements.text: What this means, and what it doesn’t

As a church, we will say yes to:
  • keeping the gospel at the centre of all we do
  • reminding one another of what God has done in Christ, not just what we should do
  • helping one another grow as disciples who trust, love, and obey Jesus
  • shaping our lives, and church priorities around God’s Word
  • helping others become disciples of Jesus

And as a church, we will say no to:
  • treating Christianity as mainly about our effort
  • moving on from the gospel to ’deeper’ things
  • making church about comfort or entertainment
  • measuring success only by numbers or busyness
  • leaving discipleship to a few leaders instead of us all sharing in it
Questions for Further Reflection

elements.text: Next Steps

• Practise explaining the gospel out loud in 2–3 minutes, until you feel confident with it.
  • Pray this week for one person you’d love to hear the good news.
  • Reflect on which part of the church vision excites you most—and share it with another member.

Prayer is not overcoming God’s reluctance, but laying hold of His willingness.
— Martin Luther

The Church is the only society that exists for the benefit of those who are not its members.
— William Temple

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Many people think of prayer primarily as asking God for things – a request line to heaven, most useful in emergencies. Others think of it as a spiritual discipline for advanced Christians, requiring the right posture, vocabulary, or length of time.


But at its simplest, prayer is honest conversation with God. It includes asking, but also thanking, confessing, listening, and simply being in his presence. Martin Luther’s point – that prayer is ’laying hold of God’s willingness, not overcoming his reluctance’ – captures something important: God is not a reluctant bureaucrat. He invites us to bring everything to him (Philippians 4:6).


The goal is not impressive prayer. It is real prayer.

elements.question:
What do you think of the above quotations?
(Do you like them or not, and why?)
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage

elements.text: Pray Always
Prayer is not just an optional extra; it is the lifeblood of the church. We depend on God for everything; prayer is how we express that.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Always rejoice, <sup>17</sup>constantly pray, <sup>18</sup>in everything give thanks. For this is God’s will for you in Christ Jesus.

elements.question: What does it mean to ’pray continually’?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Praying continually does not mean being on our knees every minute, but living with an ongoing awareness of God’s presence and a habit of turning to him in all situations – in gratitude, in need, in uncertainty. The three commands here (rejoice always, pray continually, give thanks in everything) are all linked and describe the same posture of a life oriented toward God. Paul calls this ’God’s will for you in Christ Jesus’, which means prayer is not an optional spiritual extra but the intended pattern of Christian life.
elements.questionHint: Paul gives three short commands in a row – how do rejoicing, praying, and giving thanks relate to each other, and what do they together suggest about the posture of a praying life?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>Do not be anxious about anything. Instead, in every situation, through prayer and petition with thanksgiving, tell your requests to God. <sup>7</sup>And the peace of God that surpasses all understanding will guard your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus.

elements.question: What should we do with our worries? What does God promise?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Instead of being anxious, we are to bring every concern to God through prayer and petition, combined with thanksgiving. The thanksgiving is significant – it reminds us of who we are praying to and what he has already done, which reframes our worries. God’s promise in response is not that circumstances will change, but that his peace – which goes beyond rational explanation – will guard our hearts and minds. Prayer does not necessarily remove the situation; it changes how we carry it, because we are no longer carrying it alone.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to pray ’with thanksgiving’ – why do you think gratitude is included alongside bringing requests, and what exactly does God promise will happen as a result?

elements.passageText: <sup>42</sup>They were devoting themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer.

elements.question: What role did prayer play in the early church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Prayer was one of the four core activities to which the early church devoted themselves, alongside the apostles’ teaching, fellowship, and breaking bread. The word ’devoting’ is important – this was not occasional or passive but consistent and intentional. Prayer sat at the heart of community life rather than being an add-on. This tells us that from the very beginning, the church understood that it could not live or grow on human effort alone; it needed to be in constant communication with God.
elements.questionHint: The verse lists four things the early church devoted themselves to – what does it mean to ’devote’ yourself to something, and what does it say about the priority they gave to prayer?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word in a church context?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
In church settings, ‘sacrifice’ can sound like giving up things you want in order to earn God’s approval – as if the more it hurts, the more it counts. This turns sacrifice into a performance.


But Romans 12:1 reframes the idea completely. The ’living sacrifice’ Paul describes is not grim self-denial – it is a response to God’s mercy. We give ourselves because we have already been given to. The sacrifice flows from gratitude, not from an attempt to earn favour.


Joyful and sacrificial are not opposites. When you understand what Christ gave up for you, giving something in return stops feeling like a loss.

elements.text: Serve Joyfully & Sacrificially

Jesus calls us to serve others, not just when it is easy, but even when it costs us. True service is both joyful (because it reflects Christ) and sacrificial (because it puts others ahead of ourselves).

elements.passageText: <sup>45</sup>For even the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.

elements.question: What example did Jesus set in serving?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus set the ultimate example of service: as the Son of Man, the most important person in history, he came not to receive service but to give it – and he took that service to its maximum conclusion by giving his very life as a ransom. This tells us that true service is not about comfort or convenience; it is about putting others first even at great personal cost. Because Jesus himself served this way, his followers are called to the same pattern – and they do so as people who have themselves been ransomed by him.
elements.questionHint: Jesus uses the word ’even’ – what does that word imply about how unexpected or remarkable his choice to serve was, given who he is?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore I exhort you, brothers and sisters, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a sacrifice – alive, holy, and pleasing to God – which is your reasonable service.

elements.question: What does it mean to offer ourselves as living sacrifices?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: To offer ourselves as living sacrifices means to place our whole lives – our time, energy, abilities, and desires – at God’s disposal as an ongoing act of worship. It is not a one-off decision but a living, active, daily commitment. Paul grounds this in ’the mercies of God’ – it is a response to what God has already done, not an attempt to earn his favour. He calls it ’reasonable service’, meaning that in light of everything God has given, offering ourselves back to him is simply the fitting response.
elements.questionHint: Paul says this is our ’reasonable service’ in response to ’the mercies of God’ – what does that suggest about the motivation behind Christian service and sacrifice?

elements.passageText: <sup>13</sup>For you were called to freedom, brothers and sisters; only do not use your freedom as an opportunity to indulge your flesh, but through love serve one another.

elements.question: How should we use our freedom in Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Christian freedom is not freedom to do whatever we want (‘indulge the flesh’), but freedom to love and serve others without the burden of self-centredness or the fear of having to earn God’s approval. Paul presents freedom and service not as opposites but as deeply connected: we are most truly free when we use our freedom for others. This redefines what freedom means – it is not self-indulgence but the liberation to genuinely put others first, which is exactly what love requires.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts two possible uses of freedom in this verse – what does the contrast between ’indulging the flesh’ and ’serving one another through love’ tell you about what true freedom is for?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Therefore, if there is any encouragement in Christ, any comfort provided by love, any fellowship in the Spirit, any affection or mercy, <sup>2</sup>complete my joy and be of the same mind, by having the same love, being united in spirit, and having one purpose. <sup>3</sup>Instead of being motivated by selfish ambition or vanity, each of you should, in humility, be moved to treat one another as more important than yourself. <sup>4</sup>Each of you should be concerned not only about your own interests, but about the interests of others as well. <sup>5</sup>You should have the same attitude toward one another that Christ Jesus had,

elements.question: What attitude should shape all Christian service?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The attitude that should shape all Christian service is humility – actively considering others as more important than ourselves and attending to their interests, not just our own. Paul contrasts this with ’selfish ambition and vanity’, which are the default postures of human nature. The motivation is not duty but the rich resources of the Christian life: encouragement in Christ, the comfort of love, fellowship in the Spirit. And the ultimate model is Christ Jesus himself, who will be described in the following verses as the supreme example of humble self-giving service.
elements.questionHint: Verse 3 tells us what not to be motivated by – what two things does Paul name, and what does he say should replace them?

elements.text: Give Joyfully & Sacrificially

Giving is part of serving. But it’s not just about money; it’s about worship. When we give, we express gratitude to God, trust in his provision, and commitment to his mission. True giving is joyful (flowing from gratitude) and sacrificial (costing us something).

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>My point is this: the person who sows sparingly will also reap sparingly, and the person who sows generously will also reap generously. <sup>7</sup>Each one of you should give just as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, because God loves a cheerful giver. <sup>8</sup>And God is able to make all grace overflow to you so that because you have enough of everything in every way at all times, you will overflow in every good work.

elements.question: What kind of giver does God love?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God loves a cheerful giver – someone who gives from a willing, joyful heart rather than from reluctance or external pressure. Paul’s agricultural image of sowing and reaping suggests that generosity is not about losing something but about planting something – and that God responds to generous giving with overflowing grace. The emphasis on giving ’as decided in your heart’ means this is an internal, voluntary act of worship, not a performance for others. Giving that flows from joy, rather than obligation, reflects a heart that has grasped what God has given.
elements.questionHint: Paul says giving should be ’not reluctantly or under compulsion’ – what does that tell you about what God is most interested in when we give?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that although he was rich, he became poor for your sakes, so that you by his poverty could become rich.

elements.question: Why can we give joyfully, even when it is sacrificial?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We can give joyfully because we have already received the greatest gift imaginable: Jesus, who was infinitely rich (in glory, power, and every spiritual blessing), chose to become poor for our sake so that we could become spiritually rich. Our giving, however sacrificial, is always a response to a far greater sacrifice already made. When we truly grasp what Christ gave up for us, our own sacrificial giving becomes an act of gratitude rather than loss – we are giving from an overflow, not from an empty well.
elements.questionHint: This verse describes a great exchange – what did Christ give up, and what did we gain as a result, and how does understanding that shape how we approach our own giving?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>‘Be careful not to display your righteousness merely to be seen by people. Otherwise you have no reward with your Father in heaven. <sup>2</sup>Thus whenever you do charitable giving, do not blow a trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in synagogues and on streets so that people will praise them. I tell you the truth, they have their reward. <sup>3</sup>But when you do your giving, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing, <sup>4</sup>so that your giving may be in secret. And your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you.

elements.question: What do we learn from Jesus about giving?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus teaches that giving must be directed toward God, not toward gaining the approval or admiration of other people. When giving is performed for an audience, the human applause becomes the only reward received. But when we give in secret – with no concern for recognition – our Father who sees everything promises his own reward. This teaches us that giving is first and foremost an act between the giver and God; its motivation should be love and worship, not reputation.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says those who give to be seen by people ’have their reward already’ – what does he mean by that, and what reward is he contrasting it with?

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>‘Do not accumulate for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy and where thieves break in and steal. <sup>20</sup>But accumulate for yourselves treasures in heaven, where moth and rust do not destroy, and thieves do not break in and steal. <sup>21</sup>For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.

elements.question: What do we learn from Jesus about giving?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus teaches that earthly wealth is insecure and temporary – subject to decay and theft – while heavenly treasure is permanent and safe. Giving generously is therefore a way of redirecting investment from what is temporary to what lasts. Most profoundly, Jesus reveals that our treasure and our heart are always connected: where we put our money reveals what we most deeply value. Generous giving is not just a financial decision – it is a spiritual one that both reflects and shapes the orientation of our hearts toward God.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’where your treasure is, there your heart will be also’ – what does that tell you about the relationship between how we use our money and what we truly love?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Honor the LORD with your wealth and with the first fruits of all your crops; <sup>10</sup>then your barns will be filled completely, and your vats will overflow with new wine.

elements.question: What does it mean to honour the Lord with our wealth?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Honouring the Lord with our wealth means giving him the ’first fruits’ – the first and best portion of what we earn, not what is left over after everything else has been taken care of. This reflects a posture of trust: we give first because we believe God is our provider and that he can be trusted with the rest. The promised outcome – overflowing barns and vats – shows that God honours those who honour him; generosity toward God is never ultimately a loss.
elements.questionHint: The passage specifies ’first fruits’ rather than leftovers – what does giving first, rather than last, say about our trust in God as provider?

elements.text: What this means, and what it doesn’t

As a church, we will say yes to:
  • making prayer a normal part of our lives
  • depending on God in everything, and bringing our worries to Him
  • serving one another in practical ways—even when it is inconvenient
  • stepping forward to help, rather than waiting to be asked
  • giving generously as an act of worship and trust
  • using our time and resources to benefit others

And as a church, we will say no to:
  • treating prayer as optional
  • trying to do church life in our own strength
  • coming mainly to be served rather than to serve
  • avoiding opportunities to serve because they are difficult
  • giving only when it feels comfortable
  • holding tightly to our time, money, or comfort

  • Do one act of service for someone this week.
  • Review your giving and prayerfully consider one change.

Evangelism is just one beggar telling another beggar where to find bread.
— D.T. Niles

The church is the mission, and the mission is the church.
— Lesslie Newbigin

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
The word ’evangelism’ tends to conjure images of confrontational strangers, high-pressure tactics, or professional speakers with presentation slides. It can feel like a specialist skill that most ordinary Christians don’t have.


But the word simply means announcing good news. The D.T. Niles image is helpful here: it’s ’one beggar telling another beggar where to find bread.’ Not an expert. Not a salesperson. Just someone who has found something and wants to share it.


The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19–20) is given to all of Jesus’ followers, not a gifted few. The power to change hearts belongs to the Spirit (Acts 1:8). Our role is simply to speak clearly and trust God with the outcome.

elements.question:
What do you think of the above quotations?
(Do you like them or not, and why?)
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage

elements.text: Make Christ Known

>Jesus’ final command to His disciples was to go and make him known to the nations. Evangelism is not for a few gifted people, but for all believers – though we won’t all be involved in the same ways.

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit, <sup>20</sup>teaching them to obey everything I have commanded you. And remember, I am with you always, to the end of the age.

elements.question: What command does Jesus give to His followers?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus commands his followers to make disciples of all nations – going, baptising, and teaching. This is not a suggestion for the spiritually advanced, but a commission given to all his disciples. It is both a universal mandate (all nations) and a comprehensive one (teaching everything he commanded). Significantly, Jesus frames the command with his own authority (‘all authority has been given to me’) and closes with his constant presence – the mission is not accomplished by human effort alone but in dependence on him.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’all nations’ and ’everything I have commanded’ – what do those two words ’all’ and ’everything’ tell you about the scope of the commission?

elements.passageText: <sup>14</sup>How are they to call on one they have not believed in? And how are they to believe in one they have not heard of? And how are they to hear without someone preaching to them? <sup>15</sup>And how are they to preach unless they are sent? As it is written, ‘How timely is the arrival of those who proclaim the good news.’

elements.question: Why is proclaiming the gospel necessary?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s chain of rhetorical questions makes a logical case: people cannot call on Jesus unless they believe in him; they cannot believe unless they have heard; they cannot hear unless someone tells them; and someone will not tell them unless they are sent. The conclusion is that proclamation is an essential link in the chain – there is no other route to faith. Evangelism is therefore not an optional activity for the keen few; it is the necessary means by which God’s saving purposes reach people.
elements.questionHint: Paul builds a chain of ’how can they... unless...’ statements – what is the essential link in that chain that only human messengers can provide?

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>Therefore we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God were making His plea through us. We plead with you on Christ’s behalf: ‘Be reconciled to God!’

elements.question: How does Paul describe our role in the world?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul calls believers ’ambassadors for Christ’ – representatives of another kingdom, carrying a message that is not their own. An ambassador does not speak for themselves; they speak on behalf of the one who sent them. Paul goes further and says that God is actually making his appeal through us – which gives evangelism extraordinary dignity and weight. Our role is not to argue people into the kingdom but to extend the invitation that God himself is making: ‘Be reconciled to God.’
elements.questionHint: What does the image of an ambassador tell you about whose message we are carrying and whose authority is behind it?

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>Conduct yourselves with wisdom toward outsiders, making the most of the opportunities. <sup>6</sup>Let your speech always be username, seasoned with salt, so that you may know how you should answer everyone.

elements.question: How should our conduct point people to Christ?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul calls for two things that work together: wise conduct and gracious speech. Living wisely ’toward outsiders’ means that how we behave creates either open doors or closed ones. Making the most of opportunities means being alert and intentional, not passive. Our speech should be gracious and ’seasoned with salt’ – full of flavour, engaging, and worth listening to – so that we can answer each person appropriately rather than giving canned responses. Witness is not just what we say but how we live and engage with those around us.
elements.questionHint: Paul mentions both conduct (how we live) and speech (what we say) – why do you think both are needed, and what might happen if we had one without the other?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the farthest parts of the earth.

elements.question: What power enables believers to witness? And where should that witness spread?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The power that enables witness is the Holy Spirit – not human confidence, personality, or skill. Jesus makes clear that the disciples must wait for this power before they go, which means evangelism is fundamentally a Spirit-empowered activity. The geographic progression – Jerusalem, Judea and Samaria, the farthest parts of the earth – describes expanding circles of witness, starting close to home and moving outward without limit. This tells us that every believer is called to witness where they are, while the church’s vision extends to the whole world.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’you will receive power’ before listing where the witness will spread – what does the order of those two things tell you about the source of effectiveness in evangelism?

elements.passageText: But set Christ apart as Lord in your hearts and always be ready to give an answer to anyone who asks about the hope you possess. Yet do it with courtesy and respect,

elements.question: What attitude should we have in evangelism?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Peter describes an attitude that combines readiness (always being prepared to explain our hope), and respect (doing so with courtesy and gentleness). The foundation is internal: setting Christ apart as Lord in our hearts first. This means that witness flows naturally from a life centred on Jesus rather than being a performance we put on. The tone is neither aggressive nor apologetic, but gentle and respectful – we are sharing something we genuinely have (hope) with those who may not yet have it.
elements.questionHint: Peter says to be ready with an answer, but then qualifies how that should be done – what two words describe the manner, and why do you think the manner matters as much as the content?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this phrase?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Church planting can sound like a specialist activity for pioneering types who move to unreached places – something most ordinary church members observe and support from a distance, if at all.


But the early church grew primarily through multiplication: congregations sending out members to start new congregations (Acts 13:2–3). It wasn’t a programme managed from headquarters – it was ordinary believers scattering and gathering.


Planting new churches is not just a strategy for mission. It is the pattern of a healthy church that takes seriously the command to reach people who aren’t yet reached. Matthew 16:18 is the firm ground: Christ himself is building his church.

elements.text: Plant More Churches

The early church grew not just through addition but multiplication – by planting new churches. Church planting extends gospel witness, strengthens discipleship, and reaches communities that existing churches cannot.

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>While they were serving the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, ‘Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them.’ <sup>3</sup>Then, after they had fasted and prayed and placed their hands on them, they sent them off.

elements.question: How did the church in Antioch participate in mission?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The church in Antioch participated in mission by willingly sending out two of their best leaders – Barnabas and Saul – at the direction of the Holy Spirit. This was costly: they gave away key people from their own community. The process involved prayer, fasting, and laying on of hands, showing that this was a serious, prayerful, Spirit-led act of the whole community, not just a decision by individuals. The church didn’t just pray for mission; it sacrificially released people for it.
elements.questionHint: The church gave away two of its leaders – what does that sacrifice tell you about how seriously they took the call to reach others beyond their own city?

elements.passageText: <sup>21</sup>After they had proclaimed the good news in that city and made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, to Iconium, and to Antioch. <sup>22</sup>They strengthened the souls of the disciples and encouraged them to continue in the faith, saying, ‘We must enter the kingdom of God through many persecutions.’ <sup>23</sup>When they had appointed elders for them in the various churches, with prayer and fasting they entrusted them to the Lord in whom they had believed.

elements.question: What did Paul and Barnabas do after making disciples?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: After making disciples, Paul and Barnabas did not simply move on – they returned to strengthen and encourage the new believers, warning them that the Christian life involves hardship, not just blessing. They also appointed elders in each church, establishing local leadership and structure so the communities could continue to grow and be cared for after the missionaries left. This shows a pattern: make disciples, build them up, establish leadership, entrust them to God – not just rapid expansion but sustainable, rooted church communities.
elements.questionHint: After making disciples, Paul and Barnabas could have moved on to new places – why do you think they went back, and what two things did they do when they returned?

elements.passageText: <sup>5</sup>The reason I left you in Crete was to set in order the remaining matters and to appoint elders in every town, as I directed you.

elements.question: Why were leaders appointed in each town?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Leaders were appointed in each town to provide structure, care, and order to newly formed congregations. The fact that Paul wanted elders in every town – not just in the major cities – shows that the goal was not a few large gatherings but many rooted, led, local communities. Good leadership is essential to a healthy church: without it, things drift, people are not cared for, and the community becomes unstable. Church planting is therefore not complete when people come to faith; it is complete when those people are gathered into a well-led, well-structured local church.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to appoint elders ’in every town’ – what does that tell you about the importance of local leadership for newly planted churches?

elements.passageText: 18And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overpower it.

elements.question: Who builds the church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Jesus builds the church. He says ’I will build my church’ – it is his project, initiated and sustained by him, belonging to him. This is both a comfort and a corrective: a comfort because the church does not ultimately depend on our cleverness or effort, and a corrective because we can be tempted to treat the church as our institution to manage. The promise that even the gates of Hades will not overpower it gives confidence that no opposition, difficulty, or failure is ultimately fatal – the outcome is secure in Christ’s hands.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’I will build my church’ – whose initiative and responsibility does that make the growth of the church, and why is that encouraging when things are hard?

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>Now those who had been scattered because of the persecution that took place over Stephen went as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, speaking the message to no one but Jews. <sup>20</sup>But there were some of them, men from Cyprus and Cyrene, who came to Antioch and began to speak to the Greeks too, proclaiming the good news of the Lord Jesus. <sup>21</sup>The hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number who believed turned to the Lord.

elements.question: How did ordinary believers spread the gospel?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Ordinary believers spread the gospel simply by going about their lives after being scattered by persecution, and speaking about Jesus wherever they went. It was not a coordinated mission programme – it was people who had been displaced talking naturally about the good news. Some of them took the further step of speaking to Greeks (not just Jews), crossing a cultural barrier. The result was a great number of people turning to the Lord. This shows that the expansion of the gospel often happens through unnamed, ordinary followers of Jesus living out their faith in new places.
elements.questionHint: These people were not sent as missionaries – they were scattered by persecution – so what does their witness tell you about who is qualified to spread the gospel?

elements.passageText: <sup>20</sup>And in this way I desire to preach where Christ has not been named, so as not to build on another person’s foundation, <sup>21</sup>but as it is written: ‘Those who were not told about him will see, and those who have not heard will understand.’

elements.question: What was Paul’s missionary strategy?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Paul’s strategy was to take the gospel to places where Christ had not yet been named – pioneering into unreached territory rather than building on work others had already done. He saw his calling as pushing the frontier outward. The Scripture he quotes makes clear this was not Paul’s own ambition but the fulfilment of God’s purpose to reach those who had never heard. This strategy implies that the goal is not just to deepen existing Christian communities, but to keep extending the reach of the gospel to new people and places.
elements.questionHint: Paul says he aimed to preach ’where Christ has not been named’ – what does that tell you about his priority between strengthening existing churches and reaching new places?

elements.passageText: <sup>8</sup>For from you the message of the Lord has echoed forth not just in Macedonia and Achaia, but in every place reports of your faith in God have spread, so that we do not need to say anything.

elements.question: How can one church have an impact beyond its city?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The Thessalonian church’s faith and witness had spread far beyond their own city – to Macedonia, Achaia, and ’every place’ – simply through the reports of their transformed lives and testimony. Their impact was so remarkable that Paul says there was nothing left for him to say; the reputation of their faith preceded him everywhere. One church can have an impact beyond its city by living distinctively, speaking openly about their faith, and becoming a community that other people notice and talk about. Reputation travels.
elements.questionHint: The Thessalonians didn’t send missionaries to every place – so how did their impact spread so widely, and what does that suggest about the power of a living, visible faith?

elements.text: What this means, and what it doesn’t

As a church, we will say yes to:
  • speaking about Jesus in everyday conversations
  • inviting others to hear the gospel
  • supporting and sending church planters
  • giving time and resources to mission

And as a church, we will say no to:
  • focusing only on ourselves
  • prioritising comfort over mission
  • keeping faith private when we could speak
  • settling for growth without reaching new people

A Bible that is falling apart usually belongs to someone who isn’t.
— Charles Spurgeon

Discipleship is the process of becoming who Jesus would be if He were you.
— Dallas Willard

elements.question:
• What role does the Bible actually play in your life at the moment?
  • What do you think it’s meant to do?
  • Think about your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Many people treat the Bible as one useful religious resource among others – something to dip into for encouragement, or consult when a specific question arises. Helpful, but optional.


But 2 Timothy 3:16–17 describes Scripture as God-breathed – meaning it carries his authority and life. It is not merely a human document about God. It is God speaking. And it is described as ’useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness’ – not passive reading material, but an active force that shapes and reforms the person who engages with it seriously.


Colossians 3:16 adds a communal dimension: the Word is to ’dwell among’ us richly – reshaping not just individuals but the life of the whole church together.

elements.question:
What do you think of the above quotations?
(Do you like them or not, and why?)
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage

elements.text: Rooted in Scripture
God’s Word is our foundation. A disciple who is not rooted in Scripture will be weak and easily shaken.

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Every scripture is inspired by God and useful for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, <sup>17</sup>that the person dedicated to God may be capable and equipped for every good work.

elements.question: What does Scripture do in our lives?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Scripture, being God-breathed (carrying the very breath and authority of God), actively works in four ways: teaching (forming right understanding), reproof (identifying where we go wrong), correction (redirecting us), and training in righteousness (building godly habits and character over time). The goal of all this is practical: that God’s people would be capable and fully equipped for every good work. Scripture is not just a source of information but a formative tool that shapes the whole person for a life of obedience and usefulness.
elements.questionHint: Paul lists four things Scripture is useful for – can you identify which ones build us up versus which ones challenge or correct us, and why might we need both?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>How blessed is the one who does not follow the advice of the wicked, or stand in the pathway with sinners, or sit in the assembly of scoffers! <sup>2</sup>Instead he finds pleasure in obeying the LORD’s commands; he meditates on his commands day and night. <sup>3</sup>He is like a tree planted by flowing streams; it yields its fruit at the proper time, and its leaves never fall off. He succeeds in everything he attempts.

elements.question: What does a life rooted in God’s Word look like?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: A life rooted in God’s Word is marked by delight in God’s commands and regular, sustained meditation on them – not merely reading, but dwelling on them day and night until they shape the mind and heart. The result is described through the image of a tree planted by a stream: deeply nourished, consistently fruitful (at the proper time), and resilient (leaves that don’t fall off). Such a person is not tossed around by the influences and advice of those who disregard God, because they are drawing from a constant, reliable source of life.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist compares the godly person to a tree planted by a stream – what is it about that image that captures what regular engagement with Scripture does for a person?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and exhorting one another with all wisdom, singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, all with grace in your hearts to God.

elements.question: How should God’s Word dwell among us?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s Word should dwell ’richly’ – not sparsely, superficially, or occasionally, but abundantly and pervasively. It is addressed to a community (‘you’, plural), meaning this is not just about individual Bible reading but about the Word shaping the shared life of the church. When it dwells richly, it produces mutual teaching and exhortation (Word-shaped conversation and encouragement), and worship that comes from a heart full of grace. The Word and worship are deeply connected: a community saturated with Scripture naturally overflows in praise.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses the word ’richly’ to describe how the Word should dwell – what would it look like for the Word to dwell in a community sparsely rather than richly, and how would you notice the difference?

elements.passageText: <sup>12</sup>For the word of God is living and active and sharper than any double-edged sword, piercing even to the point of dividing soul from spirit, and joints from marrow; it is able to judge the desires and thoughts of the heart.

elements.question:
• What do you think Christian growth actually looks like?
  • Is it mainly effort, mainly waiting, or something else?
  • Think about your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
Christian growth is easily misunderstood in two opposite directions. Some treat it as a self-improvement project – try harder, behave better, fix the obvious problems. Others treat it as entirely passive – just wait for God to change you, and don’t strain yourself.


John 15:4–5 offers a better image: a branch abiding in the vine. The branch doesn’t manufacture fruit, but it isn’t passive either. It stays connected. The life flows from the vine; the branch’s job is not to strain but to remain.


Romans 12:2 adds the active side: ‘be transformed by the renewing of your mind.’ Growth involves engagement – with Scripture, prayer, community, and worship – not as ways to earn favour, but as the conditions in which the Spirit does his work.

elements.question: What effect does God’s Word have?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s Word is not a static text – it is living and active, carrying divine power. It penetrates to the deepest level of human experience, cutting through the distinction between soul and spirit, and judging the true desires and thoughts of the heart. This means God’s Word sees what we cannot see about ourselves – our real motivations, hidden idols, and self-deceptions. Engaging with it seriously is not a safe, comfortable experience; it exposes us. But this exposure is also what produces genuine transformation, because we cannot change what we cannot see.
elements.questionHint: The writer says the Word can judge ’the desires and thoughts of the heart’ – what does it mean for a text to be able to do that, and why might that be both uncomfortable and necessary for growth?

elements.text: Grow in Christ
Following Jesus is a lifelong journey of transformation. Growth means becoming more like him in our attitudes, desires, and actions.

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>Do not be conformed to this present world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, so that you may test and approve what is the will of God – what is good and well-pleasing and perfect.

elements.question: What does transformation look like?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Transformation is the opposite of being squeezed into the mould of the surrounding culture. It happens from the inside out, through the renewing of the mind – as our thinking, values, and instincts are gradually reshaped by God’s Word and Spirit rather than by the world’s assumptions. The outcome is practical discernment: a transformed person becomes able to test and recognise what God’s will is in real situations. Transformation is therefore not just a change in behaviour but a change in how we think and perceive, which then produces changed behaviour naturally.
elements.questionHint: Paul contrasts being ’conformed’ to the world with being ’transformed’ – what does the location of the change (the mind) suggest about where true transformation begins?

elements.passageText: <sup>15</sup>but speaking the truth in love, we will in all things grow up into Christ, who is the head.

elements.question: How does growth happen in the church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Growth in the church happens through the combination of truth and love: speaking honestly to one another, but doing so in love rather than harshness. This means growth is not a solo project – it requires the community. We grow ’in all things’ into Christ, who is the head, meaning the goal is Christlikeness and the measure is not our own standard but his. The implication is that a church where truth is avoided (to keep the peace) or where truth is spoken without love (harshly or critically) will not produce mature disciples.
elements.questionHint: Paul says we grow by ’speaking the truth in love’ – what happens when you have truth without love, or love without truth, and why does it take both for real growth to occur?

elements.passageText: <sup>18</sup>But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be the honor both now and on that eternal day.

elements.question: What are we urged to grow in?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We are urged to grow in two things that belong together: grace (the experience of God’s undeserved favour and the Christ-like character that flows from it) and knowledge (deeper understanding of who Jesus is and what he has done). Growth in grace without knowledge can become emotionalism; growth in knowledge without grace can become pride. Together they produce a disciple who is both well-informed and transformed in character. The doxology at the end – ‘to him be the honour’ – shows that growth is not ultimately about us but about bringing glory to Christ.
elements.questionHint: Peter pairs grace and knowledge together – why might it be important to grow in both at the same time, rather than focusing on just one of them?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>Remain in me, and I will remain in you. Just as the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it remains in the vine, so neither can you unless you remain in me. <sup>5</sup>I am the vine; you are the branches. The one who remains in me – and I in him – bears much fruit, because apart from me you can accomplish nothing.

elements.question: What brings fruitfulness?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Fruitfulness comes from remaining in Christ – staying connected to him, not straining to produce results through independent effort. Jesus uses the image of a branch and vine: a branch cannot produce fruit by itself; it must stay attached to the vine where the life is. The positive promise is striking: remaining in Christ results in bearing much fruit. The negative statement is equally stark: ‘apart from me you can accomplish nothing.’ Genuine spiritual fruitfulness – in character, in ministry, in witness – is not the product of effort alone but of abiding in Christ through prayer, Word, and worship.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’apart from me you can accomplish nothing’ – what does this tell you about the relationship between spiritual effort and spiritual connection, and which comes first?

elements.text: Growing in Christ means abiding in him daily and pressing forward with the goal of becoming mature, fruitful disciples.
Build the Next Generation
The gospel must be passed on. Building the next generation means discipling children, teens, youth, and young believers so that faith continues and multiplies.

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>We will not hide them from their descendants; we will tell the next generation about the LORD’s praiseworthy acts, about his strength and the amazing things he has done. <sup>5</sup>He established a rule in Jacob; he set up a law in Israel. He commanded our ancestors to make his deeds known to their descendants, <sup>6</sup>so that the next generation, children yet to be born, might know about them. They will grow up and tell their descendants about them. <sup>7</sup>Then they will place their confidence in God. They will not forget the works of God, and they will obey his commands.

elements.question: What responsibility do we have to the next generation?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We have a God-given responsibility to actively tell the next generation about what God has done – his praiseworthy acts, his strength, his amazing deeds. This is not optional; God commanded it. The purpose is so that each new generation would know God, trust him, and obey him – and then pass the same on to their descendants. The responsibility has a chain reaction quality: faithfulness in one generation plants the seeds of faithfulness in the next. Failing to pass on the faith is therefore not a neutral choice – it breaks a chain that God intended to continue.
elements.questionHint: The psalm describes a chain running from one generation to the next – what is the one thing in the chain that each generation must actively do to keep it unbroken?

elements.passageText: <sup>2</sup>And entrust what you heard me say in the presence of many others as witnesses to faithful people who will be competent to teach others as well.

elements.question: How is the gospel passed on?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The gospel is passed on through intentional entrusting – Paul to Timothy, Timothy to faithful people, those people to others. This is a four-generation chain in one verse: Paul, Timothy, reliable people, the people they will teach. The process is personal and relational, not just institutional. The qualifier ’faithful’ is significant: the selection criterion is not talent or seniority but trustworthiness and competence to teach. This model of multiplying discipleship through individuals who invest in others is the pattern that enabled the gospel to reach across the world.
elements.questionHint: Paul describes four generations of transmission in this one verse – what does that multiplication model suggest about the most powerful way to spread the gospel over time?

elements.passageText: <sup>6</sup>These words I am commanding you today must be kept in mind, <sup>7</sup>and you must teach them to your children and speak of them as you sit in your house, as you walk along the road, as you lie down, and as you get up.

elements.question: How should God’s Word be taught in families?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: God’s Word should be taught in families in a way that is woven into the everyday rhythms of life – sitting at home, walking, lying down, getting up. This is not primarily about formal Bible classes (though those have value) but about faith being a natural, constant part of daily conversation and activity. The implication is that children learn faith not just through scheduled instruction but by observing parents who live and talk about God’s Word as they go about normal life. Faith is caught as much as it is taught.
elements.questionHint: God describes teaching happening in four different ordinary moments of the day – what does the range of those moments suggest about where faith formation actually happens?

elements.passageText: <sup>4</sup>I have no greater joy than this: to hear that my children are living according to the truth.

elements.question: What brings joy to a spiritual parent?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The greatest joy for a spiritual parent is not the success, achievement, or comfort of their spiritual children, but hearing that they are living according to the truth. John is talking about spiritual children – those he had discipled and invested in – and his measure of joy is not their worldly flourishing but their faithfulness. This reframes what discipleship is aiming for: not that those we invest in would be impressive or successful, but that they would be walking with God. That is the deepest fruit of spiritual parenting.
elements.questionHint: John says this is his greatest joy – not that his spiritual children are successful or comfortable, but that they are ’living according to the truth’. What does that tell you about what the real goal of discipling others is?

elements.text: Building the next generation means faithfully passing on the gospel so that children and young believers grow to know, love, and follow Christ for themselves. We want to see lots of twenty-year-old deep disciples!
What this means, and what it doesn’t

As a church, we will say yes to:
  • building our lives on God’s Word
  • pursuing real spiritual growth
  • abiding in Christ daily
  • investing in the next generation

And as a church, we will say no to:
  • treating the Bible as optional
  • being content with shallow faith
  • neglecting growth
  • ignoring the next generation
Questions for Further Reflection
