• Read one chapter of Scripture each day.
  • Ask God to show you one area to grow in.
  • Pray for a young believer in our church by name.

The chief end of man is to glorify God and enjoy Him forever.
— Westminster Shorter Catechism

The church is not a gallery for the exhibition of eminent Christians, but a school for the education of imperfect ones.
— Henry Ward Beecher

elements.question:
What do you think of the above quotations?
(Do you like them or not, and why?)
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
In common usage, ‘worship’ often means the singing portion of a church service – or a particular style of music. ’I like the worship at that church’ usually means ’I like the songs.’


But the Bible uses the word far more broadly. Worship is any act that ascribes worth to God – which includes singing, but also prayer, giving, serving, gathering, and the whole of daily life offered to him (Romans 12:1). Psalm 100 calls us to ’serve the Lord with gladness’ and ’come before him with joyful songs’ in the same breath.


Revelation 7:9–10 gives the widest possible frame: an innumerable multitude from every nation worshipping together. What we do on Sunday is a foretaste of that – which changes how we approach it.

elements.text: Magnify God Together
Discipleship is not done alone. God calls his people to worship him together, lifting his name high as one body.

elements.passageText: <sup>3</sup>Magnify the LORD with me! Let us exalt his name together!

elements.question: What happens when we magnify the Lord together?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: When we magnify the Lord together, we exalt his name collectively in a way that cannot be done alone. The psalmist’s invitation – ‘magnify the LORD with me’ and ’let us exalt his name together’ – assumes a gathered community, not isolated individuals. Together, our worship is amplified and our perspective on God is enlarged: we hear what others have experienced of him, we are encouraged by others’ faith, and our individual sense of God’s greatness grows. Corporate worship is not just the sum of individual worshippers but something qualitatively different.
elements.questionHint: The psalmist uses both ’with me’ and ’together’ – what do those words suggest about why worship done together is different from worship done alone?

elements.passageText: <sup>24</sup>And let us take thought of how to spur one another on to love and good works, <sup>25</sup>not forsaking our own assembling together, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging each other, and even more so because you see the day drawing near.

elements.question: Why is gathering essential?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Gathering is essential because the Christian life requires mutual encouragement and accountability that cannot happen in isolation. The writer names specific purposes: spurring one another on to love and good works, and encouraging each other. These are relational and communal activities that require presence. The warning against ’forsaking’ gathering suggests it is a real temptation that some were already yielding to – implying that neglecting gathering has real spiritual consequences. The urgency is heightened by the approaching ’day’ (Christ’s return), which makes the community’s encouragement even more important.
elements.questionHint: The writer says we should ’take thought of how to spur one another on’ – why does spurring someone on require being present with them, and what is lost if we are absent?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and exhorting one another with all wisdom, singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs, all with grace in your hearts to God. <sup>17</sup>And whatever you do in word or deed, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him.

elements.question: How do worship and community belong together?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Worship and community are inseparable in this passage: the Word dwelling richly produces both mutual teaching (a community activity) and singing (a worship activity) in the same breath. Paul’s vision is of a community where the Word shapes relationships, and those Word-shaped relationships overflow in worship. Verse 17 extends this further: every act, in word or deed, done in Jesus’ name with thanksgiving, is an act of worship. The community’s common life – how they treat one another, how they speak, how they work – is itself a form of worship.
elements.questionHint: In these verses, Paul seems to move seamlessly between teaching one another and singing to God – what does it suggest about worship that he doesn’t treat these as separate categories?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Shout out praises to the LORD, all the earth! <sup>2</sup>Worship the LORD with joy! Enter his presence with joyful singing! <sup>3</sup>Acknowledge that the LORD is God! He made us and we belong to him; we are his people, the sheep of his pasture. <sup>4</sup>Enter his gates with thanksgiving, and his courts with praise! Give thanks to him! Praise his name!

elements.question: What attitude should we bring to worship?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We should bring joy, gladness, and thanksgiving to worship – not reluctance or passive attendance. The psalm is full of active, energetic commands: shout, worship with joy, sing, acknowledge, give thanks, praise. The foundation for this attitude is theological: God made us, we belong to him, we are his people. Worship done with this understanding is not a performance or an obligation; it is a natural response to who God is and what he has done. The right attitude in worship begins with right thinking about God.
elements.questionHint: The psalm gives reasons for the joyful attitude it commands – what does verse 3 tell us about God and ourselves that forms the basis for worshipping with joy?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people of his own, so that you may proclaim the virtues of the one who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light.

elements.question: What identity do we share?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Together, believers share an extraordinary corporate identity: a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people belonging to God. Each of these titles was once applied to Old Testament Israel and is now applied to the whole people of God across every background. This identity is not earned but given – we have been called ’out of darkness into his marvellous light’. The purpose of this identity is not self-congratulation but proclamation: we exist to declare the praises of the one who rescued us, which gives the community a missional orientation at its very core.
elements.questionHint: Peter gives four titles for God’s people – and then gives a purpose (‘so that’) – what does that purpose tell you about why God has given us such a remarkable identity?

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>After these things I looked, and here was an enormous crowd that no one could count, made up of persons from every nation, tribe, people, and language, standing before the throne and before the Lamb dressed in long white robes, and with palm branches in their hands. <sup>10</sup>They were shouting out in a loud voice: ‘Salvation belongs to our God, to the one seated on the throne, and to the Lamb!’

elements.question: What does eternity involve?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Eternity involves a vast, uncountable multitude from every nation, tribe, people, and language standing before God and the Lamb in joyful, collective worship. The diversity of this vision is striking – no single culture or language dominates; all are represented before the throne. The content of their worship is the proclamation of salvation: ‘Salvation belongs to our God and to the Lamb.’ Eternity is therefore not a private spiritual experience but a great communal celebration of the rescue that God has accomplished. What the church does together in worship now is a foretaste and rehearsal of this.
elements.questionHint: The vision shows people from ’every nation, tribe, people, and language’ together in one place – what does that remarkable diversity say about the scope of God’s saving purposes?

elements.question:
• What do you think of when you hear this word?
  • What do you think it actually means?
  • Think of your own answer first, then look at ours.
elements.answer:
’Fellowship’ in church culture often means social time – coffee after the service, a shared meal, a friendly chat. These things matter. But the New Testament word (koinonia) carries considerably more weight than that.


Koinonia means participation or sharing in common. You dont just have fellowship – you have fellowship in something – or Someone(!) In Acts 2:42, the early church devoted themselves to it alongside teaching, breaking bread, and prayer – suggesting it was something more intentional than a social habit. It involves genuine mutual commitment: bearing one another’s burdens (Galatians 6:2), spurring one another on (Hebrews 10:24–25), and showing the kind of love that makes the outside world take notice (John 13:34–35).


Fellowship is not what happens after church. It is what the church is.

elements.text: Be a Big, Warm, Welcoming Family
The church is God’s household, God’s family. As disciples, we are called to love one another, care for one another, and welcome others into the family.

elements.passageText: <sup>9</sup>Love must be without hypocrisy. Abhor what is evil, cling to what is good. <sup>10</sup>Be devoted to one another with mutual love, showing eagerness in honoring one another. <sup>11</sup>Do not lag in zeal, be enthusiastic in spirit, serve the Lord. <sup>12</sup>Rejoice in hope, endure in suffering, persist in prayer. <sup>13</sup>Contribute to the needs of the saints, pursue hospitality.

elements.question: What should mark our relationships?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Our relationships should be marked by sincere, unhypocritical love – the kind that cannot be faked. Paul describes devotion to one another, eagerness to honour others above ourselves, and genuine enthusiasm that does not grow lazy. There is also a strongly practical dimension: contributing to the needs of others, and actively pursuing hospitality (not just accepting it when convenient). This is not casual friendliness but a deep, energetic, costly love that imitates the character of God and serves others regardless of convenience.
elements.questionHint: Paul says love ’must be without hypocrisy’ – what is the difference between genuine love and the kind of surface-level friendliness that could look similar but isn’t the same thing?

elements.passageText: <sup>34</sup>I give you a new commandment – to love one another. Just as I have loved you, you also are to love one another. <sup>35</sup>Everyone will know by this that you are my disciples – if you have love for one another.

elements.question: What proves we are Jesus’ disciples?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: What proves we are Jesus’ disciples is not our doctrine, our church attendance, or our moral performance, but love for one another that is visible enough to make outsiders take notice. Jesus says ’everyone will know’ – meaning this love is observable, public, and distinctive. The standard of this love is high: ‘just as I have loved you’, which is self-giving, sacrificial, and unconditional. The fact that Jesus calls this a ’new’ commandment suggests it is meant to be the defining characteristic of the Christian community, not one virtue among many.
elements.questionHint: Jesus says ’everyone will know by this’ – what does the word ’everyone’ tell you about how visible and noticeable the love of Jesus’ disciples is meant to be?

elements.passageText: <sup>19</sup>So then you are no longer foreigners and noncitizens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of God’s household, <sup>20</sup>because you have been built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus himself as the cornerstone. <sup>21</sup>In him the whole building, being joined together, grows into a holy temple in the Lord. <sup>22</sup>In him you also are being built together into a dwelling place of God in the Spirit.

elements.question: What is our identity?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Our identity is as members of God’s household and fellow citizens with all who belong to him – no longer outsiders or strangers. We are built together on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ as the cornerstone who holds everything in alignment. Together, we are being formed into a holy temple, a dwelling place for God in the Spirit. This means the church is not a human organisation – it is the place where God lives among his people. Our identity is therefore both humbling (it is all God’s doing) and astonishing (we are where God dwells).
elements.questionHint: Paul says we are no longer ’foreigners and noncitizens’ – who were the people he was originally writing to, and why would this change in status have been so remarkable for them?

elements.passageText: <sup>42</sup>They were devoting themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer. <sup>43</sup>Reverential awe came over everyone, and many wonders and miraculous signs came about by the apostles. <sup>44</sup>All who believed were together and held everything in common, <sup>45</sup>and they began selling their property and possessions and distributing the proceeds to everyone, as anyone had need. <sup>46</sup>Every day they continued to gather together by common consent in the temple courts, breaking bread from house to house, sharing their food with glad and humble hearts, <sup>47</sup>praising God and having the good will of all the people. And the Lord was adding to their number every day those who were being saved.

elements.question: What marked the early church?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: The early church was marked by devotion to four things: apostolic teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, and prayer. But beyond those commitments, it was marked by an extraordinary generosity and togetherness: they held possessions in common, sold property to meet needs, gathered daily, and shared meals with glad and humble hearts. The community inspired awe in those around them, and the Lord kept adding new believers. What is striking is that this radical love and generosity was the natural outflow of their devotion to God – it did not have to be manufactured; it was the fruit of genuine transformation.
elements.questionHint: The passage describes a community that was growing rapidly – which aspects of their shared life do you think made them most attractive to outsiders, and why?

elements.passageText: <sup>10</sup>So then, whenever we have an opportunity, let us do good to all people, and especially to those who belong to the family of faith.

elements.question: Who should we care for?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We should care for all people – the call to do good has a universal scope. But Paul acknowledges a particular responsibility to those within the family of faith (‘especially’). This is not an excuse to ignore outsiders, but a recognition that the church has a specific covenant commitment to one another. The word ’opportunity’ is also significant: care is not only for emergencies but for every situation in which we can do good. Practical love should be a constant orientation, not a periodic programme.
elements.questionHint: Paul says to do good to ’all people’ and ’especially’ to the family of faith – what does that priority order suggest about the relationship between care within the church and care for the wider world?

elements.passageText: <sup>11</sup>Therefore encourage one another and build up each other, just as you are in fact doing.

elements.question: What responsibility do we have?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: We have a mutual responsibility to encourage and build up one another. This is not the job of leaders alone – the instruction is given to the whole community, using ’one another’ language that implies everyone participating. Paul’s affirmation (‘just as you are in fact doing’) shows this is not an unattainable ideal but a real practice he had seen in this community. Encouragement and building up are active, intentional choices: they do not happen by accident but require that we pay attention to one another and take responsibility for each other’s growth and wellbeing.
elements.questionHint: Paul uses ’one another’ language – what does that phrase tell you about whether this responsibility belongs to leaders only, or to every member of the community?

elements.passageText: <sup>1</sup>Brotherly love must continue. <sup>2</sup>Do not neglect hospitality, because through it some have entertained angels without knowing it.

elements.question: How should we show hospitality?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Hospitality should be shown as a consistent, non-negotiable part of Christian life – the writer says it must not be neglected, implying it is a duty, not a gift some people have. The reference to entertaining angels without knowing it (an echo of Abraham in Genesis 18) suggests that every act of hospitality carries more significance than it appears; we cannot always see whom we are welcoming or what God is doing through it. True hospitality is open-handed and unprejudiced – welcoming the stranger, not just those who are easy or familiar.
elements.questionHint: The writer says ’some have entertained angels without knowing it’ – what does the ’without knowing it’ part suggest about the spirit in which hospitality should be offered?

elements.passageText: <sup>16</sup>We have come to know love by this: that Jesus laid down his life for us; thus we ought to lay down our lives for our fellow Christians. <sup>17</sup>But whoever has the world’s possessions and sees his fellow Christian in need and shuts off his compassion against him, how can the love of God reside in such a person? <sup>18</sup>Little children, let us not love with word or with tongue but in deed and in truth.

elements.question: How should love be expressed?
elements.answerPlaceholder: Answer the question from the passage
elements.sampleAnswer: Love should be expressed in deed and truth, not merely in words or feelings. John’s standard is high: just as Jesus laid down his life for us, we ought to lay down our lives for one another. He then applies this concretely: if we have material possessions and see a fellow believer in need but close our hearts to them, the love of God simply cannot be present in us. This is a diagnostic test: the way we use what we have toward those in need reveals whether our love is genuine or merely verbal. Real love costs something.
elements.questionHint: John says love must be ’in deed and in truth, not word or tongue’ – what is the difference between those two kinds of love, and how does verse 17 give a concrete example of that difference?

elements.text: What this means, and what it doesn’t

As a church, we will say yes to:
  • gathering regularly to worship God
  • participating actively in worship
  • building loving relationships
  • welcoming newcomers
  • showing practical care

And as a church, we will say no to:
  • treating church as optional
  • being passive spectators
  • keeping relationships shallow
  • ignoring those in need
  • limiting love to words
Questions for Further Reflection

studyOnboardingSlides.heading: How to use this app
studyOnboardingSlides.body: If you want a full guide to getting the most from this app and this study – whether on your own or in a group – you can tap the button below.

studyOnboardingSlides.bodyAfter: You can also find it any time via the ? icon in the top navigation bar.

blocks.title: New members and those considering membership
blocks.body: This study is designed for people joining Berean Bible Church, or thinking about doing so. It works through the church’s vision — Make Many Deep Disciples — one word at a time, grounding each part of that vision in Scripture. By the end, you will have a clear picture of what this church is for, what it is asking of you, and why.

blocks.title: Existing members wanting a firmer foundation
blocks.body: If you have been part of the church for a while but have never worked through its vision in a structured way, this study is worth doing. It is easy to be active in a church without really knowing why it does what it does. These five sessions give you the biblical foundations — not just what the church believes, but where those convictions come from.

blocks.title: Leaders and disciplers
blocks.body: If you are leading others through this course — whether as a small group leader, a pastor, or a mentor — the material is designed to prompt honest reflection rather than just correct answers. The questions surface what people actually think, which is often more valuable than the right answer given too quickly.

blocks.title: On your own
blocks.body: This study works well as a personal study. Work through each chapter at your own pace, read the Bible passages, write your answers to the questions, and spend time with the reflection questions at the end. There is no right speed — some chapters may take twenty minutes, others longer if a question stops you in your tracks.

blocks.title: In a group
blocks.body: The study also works well in a small group or membership class. You could work through each chapter together from scratch, reading the passages and discussing the questions as you go. Alternatively — and this is often the most rewarding approach — each person works through the chapter individually first, writes their answers, and then the group comes together to discuss what they found. Either way works.

blocks.title: Working with a leader or mentor
blocks.body:
It can make a real difference to have someone guiding you through this course — a pastor, a small group leader, or a more experienced church member who can help you think through your answers and questions. Perhaps they are the one who recommended this study to you in the first place.

At the end of each chapter, the Share button lets you send your written answers directly to your leader or mentor — via WhatsApp, email, or any other app on your phone. This gives them a window into your thinking before you meet, and makes for much richer conversation.

blocks.title: Two kinds of questions
blocks.body: Each chapter has two kinds of questions. The Bible questions are tied to specific passages — asking you to look carefully at what the text actually says. The reflection questions at the end are more personal — asking what the chapter means for you specifically, and what you want to carry into your week.

blocks.title: Common Misunderstanding cards
blocks.body: Throughout the study you will encounter darker-coloured cards headed Common Misunderstanding. These address words — like gospel, grace, evangelism, or worship — that Christians use all the time but that are often misunderstood. Take a moment to think about what you understand by the word, then tap Find out to see a short clarification.

leadersNotesData.subtitle: Make Many Deep Disciples
intro: These notes are designed to help group leaders and mentors get the most out of each chapter — both for the people they are leading and for themselves. They are not a substitute for working through the material yourself first. The best leaders are those who have worked through the questions personally before helping others do the same.

This study is a church membership course. Many in your group will already be believers — some mature, some newer. A few may be genuinely uncertain about their own faith. Hold that range in mind as you lead.

Keep the gospel central. Each chapter flows from the gospel outward. The temptation is to discuss strategy, preference, or church culture. Gently redirect discussions back to the scriptural foundations the study is building on.

Create a safe space for honesty. The questions are designed to prompt real reflection — including on areas where people may feel they are falling short. Your job is not to make people feel guilty, but to help them see how the gospel shapes everything.
Don’t rush the silences. Some of the best moments in a group study happen after a question lands and nobody speaks for a few seconds. Resist the urge to fill every silence.
Watch for the person who goes quiet. Sometimes the most significant thing happening in the room is with the person who says the least. Check in with them afterwards.

keyPoints.body:
This opening chapter lays the theological foundation for everything that follows. The whole study grows from the gospel, so it is worth spending real time here. If this chapter is rushed, the rest of the study will feel like a list of church values rather than a response to what God has done.


Draw out the logic: the gospel is not advice we must follow but news about what God has done. This is the starting point of genuine discipleship, and it explains why the church vision is what it is.


The church vision — a community of believers, rooted in Scripture, growing in Christlikeness, and reaching our world with the hope of the gospel — arises directly from the gospel. Help the group see that each element of the tagline ’Make Many Deep Disciples’ is not an organisational goal but a gospel response.


The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19–20) and the image of being ’rooted and built up’ (Colossians 2:6–7) are the twin pillars. Disciples who are truly rooted in Christ will naturally want to make more disciples.

pastorals.body:
Some in your group may have heard the gospel many times and assume they know it well. Encourage them to engage freshly with the passages rather than just confirming what they already think. The gospel has a way of surprising even those who have known it for years.


Others may feel that a ’church vision’ sounds institutional or impersonal. Help them see that the vision is simply a description of what Spirit-filled, gospel-shaped church life looks like — it’s the natural result of real discipleship, not a corporate mission statement imposed from above.


Be attentive to anyone who seems uncertain whether they have truly believed the gospel. The chapter covers the basics (sin, grace, faith, the death and resurrection of Christ) without being a full evangelistic study. If someone in the group is not yet a Christian, this chapter may be the most significant one for them.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the tendency to move quickly past the gospel material toward the church-vision material. The study is structured so that the vision only makes sense in light of the gospel. If the group is impatient to ’get to the practical stuff’, gently note that the gospel is the practical stuff.

keyPoints.body:
This chapter addresses the disciplines of church life that make the ’Make’ happen: prayer, serving, and giving. All three flow from the same source — a heart that has received grace and now responds with dependence, generosity, and self-giving.


The key insight is that prayer, service, and giving are not burdens or membership duties but expressions of what it means to be a disciple. Mark 10:45 and Romans 12:1 together paint the picture: Jesus served sacrificially, and we are called to present ourselves as living sacrifices in response.


On prayer: push the group past the idea of prayer as asking God for things. The Philippians 4 and 1 Thessalonians 5 passages open up a vision of prayer as a whole posture of dependence — bringing everything to God and receiving his peace in return.


On giving: 2 Corinthians 8–9 grounds generous giving in the gospel (‘For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ’). Giving is a spiritual discipline, not a financial obligation. Help the group see the link between what Christ gave and what we are invited to give.

pastorals.body:
Money is a sensitive topic in almost every group. Approach the giving section with care, but don’t skip it — Jesus spoke about money more than almost any other subject, and the study’s passages are rich. The goal is not to make people feel guilty about their income or spending, but to help them see that generosity is a response to grace.


On service: some people in your group may already be over-stretched in church activities. Distinguish between guilt-driven busyness (doing more to prove yourself) and grace-driven service (responding to Christ’s love with joyful generosity of time and energy). The Philippians 2 passage on the mind of Christ is particularly useful here.


Some may feel their prayer life is inadequate. Normalise the struggle — every believer finds prayer difficult at times. The call of 1 Thessalonians 5:17 (‘pray continually’) is about an orientation of the heart, not a quota of minutes per day.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the person who is very active in church service but has never reflected on why they serve. Busyness and self-sacrifice are not the same as gospel-shaped service. The chapter’s framework — ‘joyful and sacrificial’ — is a useful prompt for honest self-examination.

keyPoints.body:
The ’Many’ chapter is about gospel growth through evangelism and church planting. It is deliberately outward-looking: the church exists not just for those already in it, but for those who are not yet part of it.


The Great Commission reappears here (Matthew 28:19–20), but now the focus is on going rather than what disciples look like internally. The question is: how do we fulfil it? The study gives two answers: by making Christ known in daily life, and by planting new churches.


Romans 10:14–15 is a key passage to slow down on. The logical chain — call on / believe / hear / preach / be sent — shows that every conversion is downstream of someone speaking and someone being sent. Help the group feel the weight of their responsibility as witnesses.


On church planting: the Acts passages show this was normal New Testament practice, not a specialist strategy. Paul did not just win converts; he established communities. Help the group see church planting as a natural expression of gospel multiplication, not just an activity for the adventurous few.

pastorals.body:
Evangelism produces guilt in many Christians who feel they are not doing enough or are not gifted for it. The 1 Peter 3:15 passage (‘always be prepared to give an answer’) is important here: it is about readiness when opportunities arise, not a quota of gospel conversations per week.


Some may have had painful experiences with aggressive or manipulative evangelism styles. Colossians 4:5–6 (‘let your conversation be full of grace, seasoned with salt’) offers a gentler, more relational model. Evangelism that flows from genuine love and friendship looks very different from a sales pitch.


Church planting may feel abstract or distant to some members — something that happens elsewhere, not here. Help the group connect their local faithfulness (inviting, welcoming, being a good neighbour) with the larger vision of multiplying gospel communities. The 1 Thessalonians 1:8 passage is encouraging: gospel impact radiates outward from ordinary faithful churches.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the temptation to turn the ’Many’ discussion into a debate about evangelism methods or church-growth strategies. The chapter is rooted in Scripture, not technique. Keep the group anchored in the passages.

keyPoints.body:
This chapter turns inward to ask: what does it mean to grow deep as a disciple? The answer comes in three movements: rooted in Scripture, growing in Christ, and building the next generation.


The Scripture passages establish that God’s Word is not optional equipment for the Christian life — it is the foundation (2 Timothy 3:16–17), the source of fruitfulness (Psalm 1), and the community’s shared treasure (Colossians 3:16). A disciple who is not in the Word will not grow deep.


The ’Grow in Christ’ section centres on abiding (John 15:4–5). The vine-and-branches image is worth spending time on: growth is not primarily about effort or activity but about connection. Disciples who abide bear fruit; those who don’t cannot. Use this to redirect any discussion away from self-improvement toward the central question of union with Christ.


The ’Build the Next Generation’ section brings in Psalm 78, Deuteronomy 6, and 2 Timothy 2:2. The common thread is intentional transmission: faith does not automatically pass to the next generation; it must be taught, modelled, and handed on. This applies to parents, to Sunday school teachers, to older members who disciple younger ones.

pastorals.body:
Some people feel guilty about how little they read the Bible or pray. Avoid making this chapter an exercise in self-condemnation. The goal is not to establish a new set of spiritual performance targets, but to help people see why these disciplines matter and to re-ignite a desire for them.


The ’next generation’ section may be particularly moving — or painful — for parents whose children have drifted from faith. Be pastoral here. The passages express a vision and a calling; they are not a verdict on anyone’s parenting. Psalm 78 is about communal faithfulness, not just family devotions.


For those who do not have children, or whose children are grown, help them see that ’building the next generation’ is not only for parents. Every older believer in the church has a role in the lives of younger ones. 2 Timothy 2:2 is addressed to Timothy (who was young and had no children in the congregation) — it’s a call to invest in anyone who will carry the faith forward.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the discussion of ’growing in Christ’ becoming a list of spiritual disciplines (quiet time, Bible reading, fasting, etc.) without the relational core. The vine-and-branches image keeps the focus where it belongs: on connection to Christ, out of which all genuine growth flows.

keyPoints.body:
The final chapter asks: what does it look like to live as disciples together? The answer comes in two movements: magnifying God together in worship, and being a big, warm, welcoming family. Both are communal and both are costly.


The worship section (Psalm 34:3, Hebrews 10:24–25, Colossians 3:16–17) shows that gathered worship is not optional for disciples — it is the regular, intentional act of exalting God together. Help the group see that Hebrews 10:25 (‘not giving up meeting together’) is written in the context of mutual encouragement and provocation to love and good deeds. Attendance matters, but so does active participation.


The vision of 1 Peter 2:9 (‘a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation’) and Revelation 7:9–10 (the great multitude) gives worship its cosmic dimension. The church gathered on Sunday is a foretaste of something much larger.


The ’Big, Warm, Welcoming Family’ section is grounded in the identity of the church as God’s household. Acts 2:42–47 paints the picture in vivid strokes. John 13:34–35 makes the stakes clear: love among disciples is Jesus’ chosen apologetic to the world.

pastorals.body:
This chapter is a natural place for honest conversation about what our church is actually like as opposed to what we want it to be. Be open to that conversation, but keep it constructive. The goal is not to catalogue failures but to let the biblical vision re-ignite desire and call people to action.


For those who are newer to the church, the ’family’ language may feel aspirational rather than actual — they may not yet feel warmly welcomed. This is worth acknowledging honestly. It is also a gentle challenge to those who have been around longer: warmth and belonging don’t happen by accident. Someone has to take the initiative.


Hebrews 13:1–2 (‘do not forget to show hospitality to strangers’) and 1 John 3:16–18 (‘let us not love with words or speech but with actions and in truth’) are good passages to focus on for the practical dimension. Love that costs us something is the measure.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the tendency to treat this chapter as a feel-good celebration of community rather than a demanding call to self-giving love. John 13:34–35 and 1 John 3:16–18 both set a high bar. The chapter is warm in tone, but the standard it describes — love as Christ loved — is searching.

paras: Rick Creighton grew up in Northern Ireland and studied maths at Oxford and then taught maths at a secondary school in England. After that, he studied theology at Moore Theological College in Sydney and London School of Theology.

He served as a church pastor in England for ten years, before moving to Nigeria in 2012, where he has been teaching at ECWA Theological Seminary Kagoro and Jos ECWA Theological Seminary. He has been involved with BeaconLight Training since 2003.
Rick is married to Alanna. They have three children: Asha, Conor, and Jack.
